title: Yeasted flatbreads
subtitle: Soft, pillowy flatbreads cooked on a dry pan
excerpt: Eight soft flatbreads made from a simple yeasted dough with a short 30-minute rest. Cooked dry on a cast-iron pan or heavy frying pan, they puff, char slightly, and stay pliable. Ready from start to finish in under an hour.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the griddle bread section documents simple yeasted and unleavened flat breads cooked without an oven. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg: griddle-cooked flat breads appear throughout as a straightforward household bread. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): simple yeasted flat breads are documented across the North of England and Wales as griddle cake variants. The recipe here is a straightforward yeasted flatbread at 60% hydration, suitable for everyday baking without specialist equipment.

content.text: Yeasted flatbreads are among the most forgiving bakes in this collection. The dough is a straightforward mix of flour, yeast, salt, and warm water; it rests for 30 minutes to let the yeast work and the gluten relax; it rolls thin and cooks in under 2 minutes a side on a dry hot pan. The short proof is enough to give the flatbreads a little lift and a softer texture than an unleavened flatbread, without the time commitment of a full risen loaf.

Cook them one at a time in a cast-iron pan as hot as you dare. The high heat creates the charred spots that give flatbreads their characteristic blistered surface. Keep the cooked ones warm under a tea towel while the rest cook; the trapped steam keeps them soft.

items.name: Water
items.prepNote: lukewarm, should feel comfortably warm, not hot

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Pour in the {{water}} lukewarm water and the {{olive-oil}} if using. Mix with a fork or your hand until a rough dough forms, then turn out and knead for 5 minutes by hand until smooth. The dough should be soft but not sticky. It should pull from the surface cleanly. Cover with a damp tea towel and leave at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces. Keep the unrolled pieces covered while you work. On a lightly floured surface, roll each piece to a circle about 20 cm across and 3 mm thick. If the dough springs back and won't stay thin, give it a short
of 5 minutes, the gluten is tight and needs a moment to relax.

Heat a cast-iron pan over high heat until very hot, a drop of water dropped in should sizzle and evaporate immediately. Cook each flatbread dry, without any oil, for 1 to 2 minutes per side. The flatbread should puff in patches and develop dark charred spots on both sides. Adjust the heat as needed: too low and the bread steams rather than chars; too high and it burns before the centre cooks through. Stack the cooked flatbreads under a tea towel as you go.
